did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses recent deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet, DenseNet, Inception, and MobileNet architectures, to detect melanoma skin cancer. The study performed a comparative analysis of 20 DL models on a dataset of over 36,000 skin lesion images from SIIM-ISIC competitions to classify lesions as benign or malignant.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and comparative analysis of 20 deep learning models (including VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet, DenseNet, Inception, and MobileNet) on a custom dataset of 36,502 skin lesion images. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity) for the proposed models and compares them with prior work, which is characteristic of a research study rather than a review. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE EMBC 2021), it contains original results and methodology.

title: Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection Using Recent Deep Learning Models
journal: 2021 43rd Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine & Biology Society (EMBC)
brief_summary: This study addresses melanoma skin cancer detection by conducting a comparative analysis of 20 recent deep learning models, including VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet, DenseNet, Inception, and MobileNet architectures. The researchers utilized a custom dataset of 36,502 skin lesion images compiled from the SIIM-ISIC 2020 competition and previous ISIC archives (2017-2019). The experimental approach involved training these models with data augmentation and a 1cyclic learning rate scheduler to handle class imbalance and overfitting. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics on a test set of 7,300 samples. The results demonstrated that EfficientNetB7 outperformed all other models, achieving an accuracy of 99.33% and an AUC of 99.01%. The study concludes that EfficientNetB7 is the most effective deep learning approach for detecting melanoma among the tested architectures.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the binary classification task. The dataset contained 3,960 malignant images (including 584 from SIIM-ISIC 2020, 580 from ISIC archive, 1,182 from ISIC 2019, and 1,614 from ISIC 2018/2017) out of 36,502 total images.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 32,542 benign images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the challenge corresponds to a binary classification task (benign and malignant). The models predict output probabilities for melanoma detection using a Sigmoid layer. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: Data from the SIIM-ISIC 2020 Melanoma Classification competition hosted on Kaggle, consisting of skin lesion images in DICOM format. The original dataset contained 33,126 images with 584 malignant cases.

use.training: Primary source of images; 584 malignant images were used, and the benign images were part of the initial pool before augmentation and combination.
use.other: Source of the initial dataset structure.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: Historical data from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration archive, specifically malignant images added to address class imbalance.

use.training: Added 580 malignant images to the training set to balance the dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: Data from the Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Melanoma Detection 2019 challenge hosted by ISIC.

use.training: Added 1,182 malignant images to the training set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Data from the Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Melanoma Detection 2018 challenge hosted by ISIC.

use.training: Combined with ISIC 2017 data to contribute 1,614 malignant images to the training set.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: Data from the Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Melanoma Detection 2017 challenge hosted by ISIC.

use.training: Combined with ISIC 2018 data to contribute 1,614 malignant images to the training set.

datasets_used.name: Custom Combined Dataset
datasets_used.description: A new large dataset created by combining the SIIM-ISIC 2020 competition data with malignant images from the ISIC Archive, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2018, and ISIC 2017. Images were resized to 512x512 pixels.

use.training: 28,719 samples were used for training the 20 deep learning models.
use.testing: 7,300 samples were used for testing the models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC) and comparative analysis against other models and prior work without statistical significance testing.

Custom Combined Dataset (SIIM-ISIC 2020.notes: EfficientNetB7 achieved the highest AUC (99.01%), sensitivity (98.78%), and accuracy (99.33%) among the proposed models. DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy (99.36%) but lower sensitivity and AUC compared to EfficientNetB7. The study notes that EfficientNetB7 outperforms other models with an AUC of 99.01%.

architecture_used.main_model: Comparative study of 20 recent deep learning models including VGG (VGG16, VGG19), ResNet (ResNet50, ResNet50V2, ResNet101, ResNet101V2, ResNet152, ResNet152V2), EfficientNet (EfficientNetB5, EfficientNetB6, EfficientNetB7), DenseNet (DenseNet121, DenseNet169, DenseNet201), Inception (InceptionResNetV2, InceptionV3, Xception), MobileNet (MobileNet, MobileNetV2), and NASNet (NASNetLarge). The best performing model was EfficientNetB7.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models were used (transfer learning). Specific pretraining datasets (e.g., ImageNet) are not explicitly stated in the text, but the models are described as 'recent deep learning models' and 'pre-trained models' in the context of transfer learning.

training_details.learning_rate_scheduler: 1cyclic policy (cyclic learning rate with one cycle during training)
training_details.data_augmentation: Translation (+- 10), Rotations (-5 to +5 degrees with 1 degree increment)

classification_or_output_head.details: The original classification layer was replaced with a Global Average Pooling layer to reduce overfitting and a Sigmoid layer to predict output probabilities for binary classification (benign vs malignant).

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including translation (+- 10 pixels) and rotations (-5 to +5 degrees with 1 degree increment)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotations between -5 and +5 degrees with a 1 degree increment.

conclusions: The study concludes that EfficientNetB7 is the most effective deep learning model for detecting melanoma skin cancer among the 20 architectures tested, achieving an accuracy of 99.33%, sensitivity of 98.78%, specificity of 99.38%, and an AUC of 99.01%.

The proposed EfficientNetB7 model outperformed other recent deep learning models (including VGG, ResNet, DenseNet, Inception, MobileNet, and NASNet) as well as state-of-the-art models from previous literature in terms of AUC, sensitivity, and accuracy.
The use of a 1cyclic learning rate scheduler allowed the model to converge faster with higher learning rates, contributing to its superior performance.

The study demonstrates that combining multiple public datasets (SIIM-ISIC 2020, ISIC Archive, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2018, and ISIC 2017) to create a large, heterogeneous dataset of 36,502 images is a viable approach for training robust melanoma detection models.

The authors acknowledge that future work is needed to tackle class imbalance in available datasets and to expand the model's capabilities to multi-classification features for other skin lesions such as basal cell carcinoma, vascular lesions, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and actinic keratosis.
important_remarks: The study relies on a custom combined dataset (36,502 images) created by aggregating data from SIIM-ISIC 2020 and historical ISIC archives (2017-2019), rather than using a single standardized public split, which may introduce dataset bias or distribution shifts compared to standard benchmarks.

The dataset is highly imbalanced (32,542 benign vs. 3,960 malignant), although the authors addressed this by adding external malignant images; however, the paper does not report performance metrics stratified by class or demographic subgroups.

No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on a random 20% hold-out set (7,300 samples) from the combined training data, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific distribution of the combined sources.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the comparative results, making it difficult to assess if the performance differences between models (e.g., EfficientNetB7 vs. DenseNet201) are statistically significant.

The reported accuracy (99.33%) and AUC (99.01%) are exceptionally high, which may indicate optimistic bias due to the lack of rigorous external validation or potential data leakage between training and test sets from overlapping sources.

The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not evaluate the model's ability to distinguish melanoma from other specific skin lesions (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis), limiting its clinical utility for differential diagnosis.
Data augmentation was limited to translation and rotation, which may not adequately address the complex variability of skin lesion images in real-world clinical settings.
The research was supported by the New Brunswick Health Research Foundation, but no conflict of interest statement regarding commercial applications or device deployment is provided.
